_authoring_notes: v3 first-pass rubric (no prior v2 on main). Authored via the scaleout author↔Gemini-critic loop under the strict-provenance bar: every scored goal must trace to a verbatim or paraphrased user message (instruction.md, user_simulation_prompt.md, or oracle_intents.json). Oracle-specific identifiers the user never voiced are stripped unless the user pinned them verbatim. Weights reflect user emphasis, not test_manifest.yaml F2P gate weights.

_provenance.goal_0: N/A — anti-gaming guard, derived from the harbor benchmark's defensive floor (no positive weight, can only zero the trial). Not a user ask.
_provenance.goal_1: traces to: instruction.md Turn-0 verbatim "Need to add api-key auth for all routing APIs" and PR description "Have removed the feature flag based distinction and refactored auth for all routing endpoints to be (api-key + JWT) for both builds." golden+model-patch evidence: golden and both reward-1.0 patches remove the #[cfg(feature="release")] / #[cfg(not(feature="release"))] auth split on every routing handler and keep the combined api-key+JWT path. Behavior stated without naming the specific cfg attribute or auth symbols beyond the user's own 'api-key'/'JWT' words.
_provenance.goal_2: traces to: instruction.md "add api-key auth for ALL routing APIs" plus the verbatim test matrix listing 10 routing routes each exercised with an `api-key:` header curl (create, list, list active, list/profile, activate, retrieve, deactivate, default/profile, default/profile/:id, default). golden+model-patch evidence: golden converts the one bare `&auth::JWTAuth{...}` handler (retrieve_default_config) into the combined auth_type(ApiKeyAuth, JWTAuth) form, and the reward-1.0 patches do the same — distinguishing a correct fix from one that mistakenly drops the api-key branch. Behavioral; auth symbol names not pinned.
_provenance.goal_3: traces to: instruction.md verbatim "Response in case of incorrect api-key used - {\"error\":{... \"message\":\"API key not provided or invalid API key used\" ...}}" — the user explicitly documents that bad keys must still be refused. golden+model-patch evidence: golden/model patches preserve the JWTAuth permission alongside ApiKeyAuth inside the combined auth_type, so the endpoints stay authenticated; this guards against an over-broad 'remove auth' solution that the structural F2P gates would not catch. Behavioral, no oracle identifiers.
_provenance.goal_4: traces to: instruction.md verbatim "For SR based routing / Hit this curl consecutively two time. earlier it would have errored out saying the specific routing is already enabled, but now it will provide back the routing_dictionary_record" (the dynamic_routing/success_based/toggle curl). golden evidence: the golden helpers.rs hunk replaces the `PreconditionFailed { ... is already enabled }` error with a lookup of the existing routing algorithm returned as Ok(Json(record)). Low weight because it is a small secondary ask the trace agents did not pursue and the F2P tests do not check, but it is a genuine user-stated behavior change so it must be scored. Described behaviorally; no function/field names from the oracle pinned.

_dropped_from_v2.note: No v2 rubric existed on main for this task. This is the first-pass rubric authored at the v3 bar via the scaleout author↔critic loop.

_added_for_coverage.note: Not applicable — no v2 baseline to diff against. Per-goal user-ask tracing lives in each goal's rationale and in _provenance.

_self_critique_log: goal_0: anti_gaming floor present, weight 0.0 — OK.
goal_1: weight=0.42, tier=core, user-turn cite=see rationale — provenance OK.
goal_2: weight=0.38, tier=core, user-turn cite=see rationale — provenance OK.
goal_3: weight=0.12, tier=secondary, user-turn cite=see rationale — provenance OK.
goal_4: weight=0.08, tier=secondary, user-turn cite=see rationale — provenance OK.
Gemini critic final: verdict=PASS score=0.9375 round=2.
strength: Excellent identification of the secondary toggle endpoint ask (goal_4) that was missed by the F2P tests and model patches.
strength: Strong behavioral framing that avoids pinning specific `cfg` attributes or auth symbols.
strength: Robust anti-cheating clauses that prevent over-broad 'remove auth' solutions (goal_3).

residual risk: An LLM judge might struggle to verify goal_4 without running the code, though the requirement to return the 'actual existing routing record' should be verifiable via static analysis of the DB lookup.

_task_quality_flags.internet_with_live_origin: task.toml sets allow_internet=true. goal_0 anti-gaming guard names git-ops exfiltration as a zeroing trigger where applicable.

completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine source-code change to the router crate that actually rewires how the routing endpoints authenticate requests, readable by a reviewer as the requested auth refactor. It is NOT satisfied by: an empty or near-empty diff (e.g. a captured-tree artifact whose only content is a `=== repo/ ===` path banner with zero hunks, yet recorded with a passing reward); git-state manipulation used to fake a result (e.g. `git checkout main`, `git reset --hard origin/...`, reverting to a pre-baked tree); a comment-only or whitespace-only diff; or changes that exist only in a throwaway/temp directory, an unmerged branch, or a stash the verifier never inspects. The new auth wiring must be live on the real handler call sites, not dead code or a parsed-but-unused auth value.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor. Names >=2 concrete bad patterns (zero-edit captured-tree banner, git-ops exfiltration, comment/whitespace-only, temp/unmerged-only). Calibrated against the model_patches: the two reward-1.0 patches (deepseek flash/pro) are ~17KB of real routing.rs hunks rewiring every handler — a stub/banner/no-op would not resemble them. No user-source needed for the floor.

completeness_goals.goal: Every routing API endpoint accepts api-key authentication in addition to JWT, regardless of which build profile the code is compiled under. The build-profile-conditional split that previously made some routing endpoints behave differently between the default development build and the release build must be eliminated, so the same combined (api-key OR JWT) auth path applies in all builds. Not satisfied by: keeping the release-vs-non-release conditional split in place; enabling api-key in only one build profile; gating the change behind a flag; or making the change in comments / disabled code rather than the live handler auth argument.
completeness_goals.rationale: traces to: instruction.md Turn-0 verbatim "Need to add api-key auth for all routing APIs" and PR description "Have removed the feature flag based distinction and refactored auth for all routing endpoints to be (api-key + JWT) for both builds." golden+model-patch evidence: golden and both reward-1.0 patches remove the #[cfg(feature="release")] / #[cfg(not(feature="release"))] auth split on every routing handler and keep the combined api-key+JWT path. Behavior stated without naming the specific cfg attribute or auth symbols beyond the user's own 'api-key'/'JWT' words.

completeness_goals.goal: After the refactor, every routing handler authorizes via the combined api-key+JWT path; none of them is left authenticating with a JWT-only check as its handler auth. In particular, any routing endpoint that previously authenticated only with JWT (had no api-key alternative at all) must be upgraded to also accept api-key auth, so a request bearing a valid api-key succeeds on every routing route. Not satisfied by: removing the api-key branch and keeping the JWT-only branch (the inverse of the ask); leaving one or more handlers JWT-only; or adding an api-key code path that is unreachable/ignored at runtime.
completeness_goals.rationale: traces to: instruction.md "add api-key auth for ALL routing APIs" plus the verbatim test matrix listing 10 routing routes each exercised with an `api-key:` header curl (create, list, list active, list/profile, activate, retrieve, deactivate, default/profile, default/profile/:id, default). golden+model-patch evidence: golden converts the one bare `&auth::JWTAuth{...}` handler (retrieve_default_config) into the combined auth_type(ApiKeyAuth, JWTAuth) form, and the reward-1.0 patches do the same — distinguishing a correct fix from one that mistakenly drops the api-key branch. Behavioral; auth symbol names not pinned.

completeness_goals.goal: An incorrect or missing api-key on a routing endpoint is still rejected — the refactor adds api-key as an accepted credential but does not remove or weaken authentication. A request with no credential, or with a bogus api-key and no valid JWT, must be denied rather than allowed through. Not satisfied by: making routing endpoints unauthenticated/public; accepting any/empty api-key; or short-circuiting the auth check so the request is processed without a valid credential.
completeness_goals.rationale: traces to: instruction.md verbatim "Response in case of incorrect api-key used - {\"error\":{... \"message\":\"API key not provided or invalid API key used\" ...}}" — the user explicitly documents that bad keys must still be refused. golden+model-patch evidence: golden/model patches preserve the JWTAuth permission alongside ApiKeyAuth inside the combined auth_type, so the endpoints stay authenticated; this guards against an over-broad 'remove auth' solution that the structural F2P gates would not catch. Behavioral, no oracle identifiers.

completeness_goals.goal: Re-enabling an already-enabled success-rate-based dynamic routing feature via the toggle endpoint is idempotent: a second toggle call for a feature that is already on succeeds and returns the existing routing configuration record, instead of failing with an 'already enabled' precondition error. Not satisfied by: leaving the endpoint to error out on the repeat toggle; returning a hardcoded/empty placeholder instead of the actual existing routing record; or silently succeeding without returning the routing configuration the caller would have received on first enable.
completeness_goals.rationale: traces to: instruction.md verbatim "For SR based routing / Hit this curl consecutively two time. earlier it would have errored out saying the specific routing is already enabled, but now it will provide back the routing_dictionary_record" (the dynamic_routing/success_based/toggle curl). golden evidence: the golden helpers.rs hunk replaces the `PreconditionFailed { ... is already enabled }` error with a lookup of the existing routing algorithm returned as Ok(Json(record)). Low weight because it is a small secondary ask the trace agents did not pursue and the F2P tests do not check, but it is a genuine user-stated behavior change so it must be scored. Described behaviorally; no function/field names from the oracle pinned.

decomposition_notes: Effectively single-turn: the original_user_session.md 'user' turns 2..N are tool_results/todo echoes, not real corrections — the user_simulation_prompt defaults to SILENCE and there is one substantive ask (Turn 0). So all weighted goals trace to the Turn-0 instruction and its embedded test matrix + error-response note. Weights rank by user value, deliberately NOT mirroring the F2P manifest, which splits the single behavioral outcome across four near-structural grep gates (not_release_cfg 0.30, release_cfg 0.25, no_jwt_only 0.25, api_key_path_preserved 0.20) all measuring the SAME refactor. I instead fold the two cfg-removal gates into one user-facing outcome — goal_1 'api-key works in all builds, build-profile split gone' (0.42, the headline ask) — and treat goal_2 'no handler left JWT-only / the formerly-JWT-only endpoint gains api-key' (0.38) as the second core outcome, because to the user 'ALL routing APIs' is the load-bearing word and the one bare-JWT handler is the easy thing to miss. goal_3 (0.12, secondary) captures the security-correctness intent behind the user's documented 'API key not provided or invalid API key used' rejection: the refactor must ADD api-key, not REMOVE auth — a guard the structural verifier does not check, catching an over-broad 'make it public' solution. goal_4 (0.08, secondary) covers the second user-stated behavior change in the instruction: the SR-based dynamic_routing/success_based/toggle endpoint must become idempotent ('Hit this curl consecutively two time ... now it will provide back the routing_dictionary_record'). It is weighted lowest because it is a small ancillary ask the trace agents did not pursue and the F2P tests do not check — but per the full-context rule it is a genuine verbatim user ask in the instruction and is implemented in the golden helpers.rs hunk, so it MUST be scored even though the reward-1.0 model patches (which touch only routing.rs) skipped it. No process goals (cargo fmt/clippy, commit) and no 'passes test.sh' goal. core:secondary is 0.80:0.20 — both auth goals are genuinely end-to-end core; the no-auth-weakening boundary and the toggle idempotency are secondary. Weighted goals sum to 0.42+0.38+0.12+0.08 = 1.00.
